epigraph.text: For my thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways my ways, declares the Lord.
epigraph.quote: The imaginary numbers are a wonderful flight of God's spirit; they are almost an amphibian between being and non-being.

content.text:
We begin our journey with a bold act of mathematical faith: we postulate 
the existence of a number $i$ with the property that $i^2 = -1$. From this 
single axiom, an entire universe of mathematics unfolds.

content.title: Complex Number
content.content:
A **complex number** is an expression of the form
\[
 z = x + iy,
\]
where $x, y \in \R$ and $i$ is the **imaginary unit** 
satisfying $i^2 = -1$. The number $x$ is called the **real part** of $z$, 
written $\re(z) = x$, and $y$ is called the **imaginary part**, written 
$\im(z) = y$.

The set of all complex numbers is denoted $\C$.

content.text:
Note that the imaginary part is a *real* number—it is the coefficient 
of $i$, not $iy$ itself. Thus $\im(3 + 4i) = 4$, not $4i$.

We define arithmetic operations on $\C$ in the natural way, treating $i$ as 
an unknown that satisfies $i^2 = -1$:

The multiplication formula follows from expanding $(x_1 + iy_1)(x_2 + iy_2)$ 
and using $i^2 = -1$. The division formula requires a technique we develop 
next.

content.title: Complex Conjugate
content.content:
The **complex conjugate** of $z = x + iy$ is
\[
 \conj{z} = x - iy.
\]
Geometrically, $\conj{z}$ is the reflection of $z$ across the real axis.

content.text:
The conjugate is surprisingly powerful. Observe what happens when we 
multiply a complex number by its conjugate:
\[
 z \cdot \conj{z} = (x + iy)(x - iy) = x^2 - (iy)^2 = x^2 - i^2 y^2 = x^2 + y^2.
\]
This is always a non-negative *real* number.

content.title: Modulus (Absolute Value)
content.content:
The **modulus** (or **absolute value**) of 
$z = x + iy$ is
\[
 |z| = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = \sqrt{z \cdot \conj{z}}.
\]

content.text:
Geometrically, $|z|$ is the distance from the origin to the point $z$ in 
the complex plane. This connects complex numbers to Euclidean geometry in 
a profound way.

content.title: Division using conjugates
Properties of the Complex Conjugate
content.content:
For all $z, w \in \C$:

1. $\conj{\conj{z}} = z$

2. $\conj{z + w} = \conj{z} + \conj{w}$

3. $\conj{z \cdot w} = \conj{z} \cdot \conj{w}$

4. $\conj{z/w} = \conj{z}/\conj{w}$ (for $w \neq 0$)

5. $z + \conj{z} = 2\re(z)$

6. $z - \conj{z} = 2i\im(z)$

7. $z$ is real if and only if $z = \conj{z}$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We prove (ii) and (iii); the others follow similarly. Let $z = x + iy$ and 
$w = u + iv$.

For (ii):
\[
 \conj{z + w} = \conj{(x + u) + i(y + v)} = (x + u) - i(y + v) 
 = (x - iy) + (u - iv) = \conj{z} + \conj{w}.
\]

For (iii):
$$
 \conj{z \cdot w} &= \conj{(xu - yv) + i(xv + yu)} = (xu - yv) - i(xv + yu) \\
 \conj{z} \cdot \conj{w} &= (x - iy)(u - iv) = xu - ixv - iyu + i^2 yv \\
 &= (xu - yv) - i(xv + yu).
$$
These are equal.

content.title: Properties of the Modulus
content.content:
For all $z, w \in \C$:

1. $|z| \geq 0$, with $|z| = 0$ if and only if $z = 0$

2. $|\conj{z}| = |z|$

3. $|z \cdot w| = |z| \cdot |w|$

4. $|z/w| = |z|/|w|$ (for $w \neq 0$)

5. $|\re(z)| \leq |z|$ and $|\im(z)| \leq |z|$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We prove (iii). We have
\[
 |zw|^2 = (zw)\conj{(zw)} = (zw)(\conj{z}\conj{w}) = (z\conj{z})(w\conj{w}) 
 = |z|^2 |w|^2.
\]
Taking square roots (all quantities are non-negative), $|zw| = |z||w|$.

content.title: Triangle Inequality
content.content:
For all $z, w \in \C$:
\[
 |z + w| \leq |z| + |w|.
\]
Equality holds if and only if one of $z, w$ is a non-negative real multiple 
of the other (including the case when one is zero).
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We compute:
$$
 |z + w|^2 &= (z + w)\conj{(z + w)} = (z + w)(\conj{z} + \conj{w}) \\
 &= z\conj{z} + z\conj{w} + \conj{z}w + w\conj{w} \\
 &= |z|^2 + |w|^2 + z\conj{w} + \conj{z\conj{w}} \\
 &= |z|^2 + |w|^2 + 2\re(z\conj{w}).
$$
Since $\re(z\conj{w}) \leq |z\conj{w}| = |z||w|$, we have
\[
 |z + w|^2 \leq |z|^2 + 2|z||w| + |w|^2 = (|z| + |w|)^2.
\]
Taking square roots gives the result.

content.text:
The name "triangle inequality" comes from the geometric interpretation: 
if we form a triangle with vertices at $0$, $z$, and $z + w$, then the 
length of any side is at most the sum of the other two.

marginNotes.text:
How precious to me are your thoughts, O God! 
How vast is the sum of them! If I would count them, they are more than the sand.

marginNotes.author: Rafael Bombelli (1572)
marginNotes.text:
Plus by plus of minus, makes plus of 
minus. Minus by plus of minus, makes minus of minus.
